county_metadata.parent_county: Marion, Darlington, Williamsburg, and Clarendon counties
county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1888.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1888 from portions of Marion, Darlington, Williamsburg, and Clarendon counties. A post-1868 county (formed after the district-to-county conversion). Named for the city that grew as a railroad junction during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Florence is a young county (1888) so pre-formation research requires checking Marion, Darlington, Williamsburg, and Clarendon county records. The Florence County Museum has genealogical materials. Doctors Bruce & Lee Foundation Library holds deed and probate abstracts. The online deed search at search.florencedeeds.com covers records from county formation. SC Judicial Department public index provides court records at publicindex.sccourts.org/florence/publicindex/. For pre-1888 records in the Florence area, start with Darlington County (formed 1785) which has the longest record run for most of the Florence territory.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds, mortgages, deeds of trust, plats, and real property records from county formation (1888) to present. Free online search portal.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Rise Up So Early: A History of Florence County, South Carolina by G. Wayne King (1981)

source_profiles.source_name: Florence County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete deed records from county formation through present. Free online portal with searchable index.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All records intact from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: SC Judicial Department Public Index — Florence County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Circuit court, probate court, and all judicial proceedings from county formation.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Estate papers, wills, and probate proceedings. Some records predate county formation because probate jurisdiction carried over from parent counties.
Florence first appears as a separate county in the 1890 census (formed 1888). 1890 census largely destroyed by fire. Pre-1888 census under Marion, Darlington, Williamsburg, and Clarendon counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records; 1890 census mostly lost nationwide.

county_metadata.record_losses: Antebellum records sent to Chesterfield for safekeeping in 1862 were destroyed by fire in March 1865 during Union occupation. Pre-1865 deeds, mortgages, and court records largely lost. Some probate abstracts survive. Marion District (1800), Horry District (1801), and Williamsburg District (1804) were all carved from Georgetown.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original colonial district dating to 1769. Converted from district to county in 1868. Parent district of Marion (1800), Horry (1801), and Williamsburg (1804). Georgetown was one of SC's most important colonial ports.
county_metadata.research_tips: This is a BURNED COUNTY — antebellum records were destroyed. Start with State Plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov for land grant substitutes. Will abstracts from will books A and B survive for 1838-1929. Georgetown County Library's Local History Room has extensive collections. The Georgetown County Historical Society is active. For plantation-era research, the Lowcountry Digital Library and Belle W. Baruch Institute for Marine & Coastal Sciences archives are valuable. Passenger lists for vessels arriving at Georgetown port survive for 1923-1939. Rice plantation records are a unique Georgetown resource — check the South Caroliniana Library at USC.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds, mortgages, plats, and land records from 1865 forward. Antebellum records lost in the 1865 Chesterfield fire. Free online search portal.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The History of Georgetown County, South Carolina by George C. Rogers Jr. (University of South Carolina Press, 1970)

source_profiles.source_name: Georgetown County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Post-Civil War deed records only. Antebellum deeds destroyed in 1865 Chesterfield fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records begin after 1865; pre-1865 records destroyed.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plat records for Georgetown District land grants. Critical substitute for destroyed county-level deed records. Part of the 51,055-image statewide collection.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-held records survived because they were not in the county courthouse.

source_profiles.source_name: Georgetown County Will Abstracts, Will Books A and B
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Will abstracts from will books A and B. Some pre-1865 probate files were also destroyed, but these abstracts survive.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Returns of marriages 1884-1899, marriage books 1911-1966.
Circuit court, probate court, and judicial proceedings post-county formation.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Excellent record survival from earliest period. Records from 1786 forward are substantially intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1786 from Ninety-Six District. Named for General Nathanael Greene. Created from Cherokee land ceded by Treaty of DeWitt's Corner (1777). Operated as a district until 1868 when SC converted districts to counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Greenville has outstanding record preservation and accessibility. The Greenville County Library's South Carolina Room has extensive genealogy collections. Greenville Historical Society holds 5,000+ historic photographs (1890s-mid 20th century). Online deed search at greenvillecounty.org/rod/ covers records back to 1786 (pre-1985 index books in one section, post-1985 all documents in another). FamilySearch has probate records (1820-1907). The Greenville County Probate Court has its own online search at greenvillecounty.org/appsas400/Probate/. Will Transcripts (1782-1855) available from SCDAH. Pinckney District Chapter of SCGS covers Greenville along with Cherokee, Spartanburg, and Union.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Pre-1985: all index books, deeds, land grants, mortgages. Post-1985: all recorded documents including deeds, plats, mortgages, liens. Two-section portal — pre-1985 and post-1985 searched separately.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Greenville: The History of the City and County in the South Carolina Piedmont by Archie Vernon Huff Jr.

source_profiles.source_name: Greenville County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Two-section portal: Pre-1985 index books, deeds, land grants, and mortgages; Post-1985 all recorded documents. One of SC's most complete online deed collections.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Will books from 1820-1907; probate court records. FamilySearch has microfilmed collections.

source_profiles.source_name: Greenville County Probate Court Online Search
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate court proceedings, wills, estates. Direct county online search portal.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Greenville District
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Searchable by name with images available.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1897.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1897 from portions of Abbeville and Edgefield counties. Both parent counties trace to Ninety-Six District. Pre-1897 records split between Abbeville (which suffered triple courthouse fires 1872-1873) and Edgefield (which famously never burned).
county_metadata.research_tips: Greenwood is a young county (1897) so pre-formation research requires checking both Abbeville and Edgefield records depending on the ancestor's location. Edgefield records are among the best-preserved in SC (never burned). Abbeville records are fragmentary due to three fires. The Greenwood County Historical Society preserves local records. Greenwood Museum features county history. Online deed search at greenwoodsc.gov/docsearchrod/. FamilySearch catalog #1152454 has marriage licenses. The Index Journal newspaper (1909-present) is a key local source.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds, mortgages, plats, and land transaction records from county formation (1897) to present.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Greenwood County: A History by Anne Herd Bowen (The Museum, 1992)

source_profiles.source_name: Greenwood County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete deed records from county formation. Free online search portal.
FamilySearch catalog #1152454. Marriage licenses from SC statewide licensing (1911).

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records, estates, wills from county formation. Pre-1897 probate under Abbeville and Edgefield.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1878.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed February 18, 1878 from northern portion of Beaufort County. Named after Confederate General and Governor Wade Hampton III. Parts later formed Jasper County (1912) and Allendale County (1919). First criminal court term held January 1879.
county_metadata.research_tips: For pre-1878 records, search Beaufort County. Hampton County Historical Society and Museum (established 1979) has local resources. 'Both Sides of the Swamp' (1970) provides county history. Parts of the county went to Jasper (1912) and Allendale (1919), so researchers may need to check those counties for post-split records. The Eighth Judicial Circuit court records are available online. Public terminal for records post-January 3, 2000 at the ROD office.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Public terminal for records post-January 3, 2000. Earlier records available on-site at Register of Deeds office. Pre-1878 records in Beaufort County.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Both Sides of the Swamp by Hampton County Tricentennial Commission (1970)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Public terminal for post-2000 records; earlier records on-site.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records, estates, wills from county formation through 1977.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation through 1966.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from district formation in 1801. Known as 'The Independent Republic of Horry' due to geographic isolation by rivers and swamps.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1801 from Georgetown District. Named for Revolutionary War hero Peter Horry (born ca. 1743). Converted from district to county in 1868. Geographically isolated by the Great Pee Dee, Little Pee Dee, and Waccamaw rivers, creating a distinctive cultural identity.
county_metadata.research_tips: Horry County has excellent online land records access through the AcclaimWeb portal (acclaimweb.horrycounty.org) going back to 1801. The Horry County Museum (est. 1979, opened 1981) has genealogical archives. The Horry County Historical Society maintains collections. Chapin Memorial Library in Myrtle Beach has genealogical resources. FamilySearch catalog #1109310 has marriage records 1911-1951. The county's isolation created distinctive community records — church records and family Bibles are particularly valuable for early period research. 'Horry County, South Carolina, 1730-1993' by Catherine Lewis and Charles Joyner is the definitive county history.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Comprehensive deed records from county formation through present. Free online AcclaimWeb portal with searchable index and document images.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Horry County, South Carolina, 1730-1993 by Catherine H. Lewis and Charles W. Joyner

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Comprehensive deed records from county formation. Free online portal with searchable index and images.
FamilySearch catalog #1109310. Marriage records on 58 microfilms.

source_profiles.source_name: Horry County Probate and Estate Records
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records, estates, wills from county formation through 1977.
Horry first appears in federal census in 1810 (formed 1801). Pre-1801 census under Georgetown District.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1912.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed January 30, 1912 from parts of Beaufort and Hampton counties. Named for Revolutionary War hero Sergeant William Jasper (ca. 1750-1779). One of SC's youngest counties. For pre-1912 records, search Beaufort and Hampton counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jasper is a young county (1912) so pre-formation research requires checking Beaufort and Hampton county records. Pratt Memorial Library in Ridgeland has 200+ portraits and maps of Lowcountry history, plus approximately 250 rare books on area history. Jasper County Historical Society holds regular meetings with presentations. Morris Center for Lowcountry Heritage is an area resource. Online deed access available through Courthouse Computer Systems portal. The Lowcountry context means Gullah-Geechee cultural heritage records are relevant.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index from county formation (1912) to present. Some limitations for pre-digital records; paper records more reliable for full title examination.

source_profiles.source_name: Jasper County Register of Deeds Online Services
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Online index incomplete for some early records; full paper records available on-site.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate court records from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census Records — Jasper County area (1920-1950)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Jasper first appears in federal census in 1920 (formed 1912). Pre-1912 census under Beaufort and Hampton counties.

county_metadata.parent_county: Camden District (from Claremont, Lancaster, Fairfield, and Richland)
county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from 1780 forward. Camden is the oldest inland city in South Carolina, settled c. 1732.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1791 from Camden District (combining portions of Claremont, Lancaster, Fairfield, and Richland). Named for Col. Joseph Kershaw (1727-1791), Revolutionary War patriot. Converted from district to county in 1868. Camden served as a major interior colonial settlement and Revolutionary War battleground.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kershaw has excellent record survival and access. Camden Archives and Museum is an outstanding genealogy resource. The online deed portal at sclandrecords.com provides deed indexes from 1791 with images beginning January 1996. FamilySearch has extensive collections: deeds with direct and cross indexes, estate records 1782-1822, equity court records 1791-1892. Will Transcripts (1782-1855) available from SCDAH. The estate record books 1782-1822 are especially valuable for early period. Camden's status as a colonial-era town means church records (Quaker, Presbyterian, Episcopal) extend back further than typical SC inland counties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Searchable deed index from 1791 to present. Scanned images begin January 1996. Plat images begin October 30, 2007. Earlier records available on index but without images.
county_metadata.heritage_book: A History of Kershaw County, South Carolina by Joan A. Inabinet and L. Glen Inabinet (University of South Carolina Press)

source_profiles.source_name: Kershaw County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete deed indexes from 1791; images begin January 1996. One of SC's deepest online deed collections.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: General index to estates and probate records; estate record books from the earliest period. Some records predate county formation from Camden District era.
Equity court records including land, probate, wills, and miscellaneous records. Equity courts handled property disputes and are a key genealogical source.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Kershaw District
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Searchable by name with images.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse burned February 1865 by Union cavalry (General Kilpatrick's forces occupied Lancaster). Most probate records lost when officials attempting to move records to safety were intercepted in open country. Equity papers destroyed. Deed records partially survived — some deed books were recovered. Pre-1865 record landscape is severely compromised but not a total loss.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed March 12, 1785 when judicial districts were divided into counties. Part of Camden District 1768-1785; early records for 1785-1800 period may be in Kershaw County (Camden District seat). Named for Lancaster, Pennsylvania — reflecting early settlers' origins. Converted from district to county in 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: This is a BURNED COUNTY — significant but not total record loss. Deed records partially survived (some deed books recovered), but probate was severely damaged. Start with the surviving deed records on lancasterscdeeds.com. FamilySearch has deed book A (1772-1788), index to deeds 1767-1937, and deed abstracts 1787-1811. Check Kershaw County for 1785-1800 Camden District records. State land grant plats at archivesindex.sc.gov provide critical pre-1865 land substitutes. FamilySearch catalog #389903 has will records. 'Historical Notes from Lancaster County, South Carolina' by Viola C. Floyd (1977) and 'Lancaster County: A Pictorial History' by Louise Pettus and Martha Bishop (1984) are key references. Louise Pettus was the county's leading historian.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds, mortgages, releases, power of attorney, restrictions, plats. Some pre-1865 deed books survived the fire and are available. Coverage strongest from 1865 forward.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Historical Notes from Lancaster County, South Carolina by Viola C. Floyd (1977); Lancaster County: A Pictorial History by Louise Pettus and Martha Bishop (1984)

source_profiles.source_name: Lancaster County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Some pre-1865 deed books survived the courthouse fire. Coverage strongest from 1865 forward. Online portal with free search.

source_profiles.source_name: Lancaster County Deed Records on FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed book A (1772-1788); index to deeds 1767-1937; abstracts 1787-1811. These surviving records are critical for pre-fire Lancaster research.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some deed books survived the 1865 fire. Abstracts and indexes provide partial reconstruction.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #389903. Will records that survived or were reconstructed after the 1865 fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial survival — most probate records destroyed when intercepted in open country during 1865 evacuation.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level land grant plats. Critical substitute for partially destroyed county deed records. Part of the 51,055-image statewide collection.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-held records — not affected by county courthouse fire.

source_profiles.repository: Kershaw County / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lancaster was part of Camden District 1785-1800; district seat records held at Kershaw County. Essential for earliest Lancaster-area land transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — held in Kershaw County, not affected by Lancaster fire.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Excellent record survival from 1785 forward.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed March 12, 1785 from Ninety-Six District along with Spartanburg, Union, Newberry, Abbeville, and Edgefield. Named for Henry Laurens, fifth president of the Continental Congress. Converted from district to county in 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: Laurens has outstanding early records. Albert Bruce Pruitt's abstracts cover the first ten deed books and first three will books (FamilySearch catalog #89965). Online deed search at search.laurensdeeds.com. FamilySearch catalog #112675 has deed records 1785-1906. The Laurens District Chapter of SCGS is active. An enrollment book of Confederate veterans (1902) survives. A database of enslaved and free persons extracted from inventory/appraisal books (1825-1839) is available. 'Discovering Laurens County, SC, Volume One' is a recent comprehensive history. Julian Stevenson Bolick's 'A Laurens County Sketchbook' (1973) and 'The Scrapbook' by Laurens County Historical Society (1982, 704 pp) are key references.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Property documents, deeds, abstracts from county formation (1785) to present. Free online search portal with Old Index Books going back to earliest records.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The Scrapbook by Laurens County Historical Society (1982, 704 pp); A Laurens County Sketchbook by Julian Stevenson Bolick (1973)

source_profiles.source_name: Laurens County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property documents, deeds, abstracts from county formation. Free online search portal.
FamilySearch catalog #112675. Deed records with index covering 121 years from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Abstracts of Early Laurens County Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #89965. Complete abstracts of first ten deed books and first three will books with name index. By Albert Bruce Pruitt.

source_profiles.source_name: Laurens County Inventory and Appraisal Database — Enslaved and Free Persons
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Database of enslaved and free persons extracted from inventory/appraisal books. Valuable for African American genealogy in the Upstate.

source_profiles.source_name: Laurens County Enrollment Book of Confederate Veterans, 1902
source_profiles.repository: Laurens District Chapter SCGS / Local Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Comprehensive enrollment of Confederate veterans from the county. Valuable for Civil War era family research.
